```{=typst}
#pagebreak(weak: true)
#wcg-nums.update(true)
#counter(page).update(1)
```

# Day 1 — Notice one spending pattern {#day1}

```{=typst}
#wcg-lead[Today has one gentle job: notice a single spending pattern — just one.]
#wcg-lesson-art("notice", caption: [Noticing one repeated choice, clearly and kindly])
```

## Why this matters

Much of what we do each day repeats in the same setting, often without a fresh
decision.^2^ These actions are triggered automatically by the cues around us — the
time of day, a familiar place, the phone in our hand.^3^ This is not a weakness; it
is how the mind saves effort.

The catch is that automatic choices are easy to overlook, so the first step is
simply to see one. Keeping track of progress toward a goal is linked with a better
chance of reaching it.^4^

## An everyday example

Think of the afternoon coffee and snack — same shop, same time, most days.
Nothing is wrong with it. But because it happens on autopilot, it is easy to never
really *look* at it. Today, you look.

```{=typst}
#wcg-action[
  Pick one purchase you tend to make without much thought — a daily drink, a
  quick add-on, a checkout snack, an app you tap by habit. When it happens,
  notice it kindly and write a few words.
]
```

> **Notice.**
>
> What did I buy today without really deciding to?

You do not need to change anything today. This is only about seeing one pattern
clearly.

{{< include assets/worksheets/ws-1-spending-pattern.qmd >}}
